item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tale about the monkey was okay. The actual skip was awful - it's a diary which rewrites an extremely narrow type of story? That's barely anything. And it's completely unconnected from the monkey story.

There are lots of articles where the interesting bits are the story surrounding the object, but even those have an anomaly that actually does something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you like it!

Yea, I meant that. About the monkey part, you can interpret it as the original writing of the monkey, or simply a fiction written by someone. In my headcanon tho, the monkey is actually sapient, and it wrote in the book, hence the bad grammar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, although I think it's a bit heavy handed.

On the "anthropomorphic non-sapient being" subject, I was confused, because the monkey in the story is clearly characterized as sapient.

Is what you meant that the 3726-1 is a sapient, anthropomophized being of a category (or species) which is typically non-sapient in reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<3 thanke me friend

The formatting is borked because I didn't know how to make it aligns automatically, so I have to manually input the margins :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Formatting is borked on mobile. Aside from that, great work as always :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the point of linking a black boxed SCP. (I'm sure most people would guess it thought taking 3009seried and desert in the same sentence).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, that was a mistake from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was afraid of that too. I will wait to see how this turns out, and I might make an external test log in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It looks like a light table, underneath something that's a really harshly contrasting color. Probably not CG'd.

also not to nitpick, but this is kinda not relevant crit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who looks at the image and thinks it looks fake as hell? Like, CG rendered or something, I swear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, but anthropomorphism doesn't [I]inherently[/I] carry sapience, does it? It's implied, but the word technically just refers to 'human characteristics' as a whole. I initially pictured anthropomorphic animals that acted like their non-anthro counterparts. You know, like Sonic the Hedgehog crawling around on all fours with glassy, non-sapient eyes?

Could do with some clarification. >__> Also yeah, in general, I feel like there might have been a mix up of -1 and -2 while writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I get the "anthropomorphic non sapient" thing.

Monkeys are not sapient (probably). Anthropomorphic monkeys are sapient, by virtue of being anthropomorphized.

So, an anthropomorphic non sapient being is actually sapient.

But yah, in the excerpts it's -2 discriminating against -1, and the description is the other way around…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot to quibble about here but I don't feel like quibbling tonight. If I come back to this in the future with nitpicks in mind, let it be known that, on initial reading, I upvoted.

My favorite part is that the monkey saw physical abuse as wrong, but killing for food as, well, just a part of life. Like, the monkey doesn't have a problem eating friends and I kind of got the impression that, if it were between the monkey starving and a friend starving, the monkey wouldn't be too sad to draw the short straw. "Eh, that's life. You better enjoy it though, buddy." But inflicting pain and injury out of anger? Big no-no in the monkey's book.

It's a really weird way to look at morality, but also pragmatic in a taboo sort of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little confused. The book originates form a conflict between - as far as I can tell - a sapient monkey and a sapient human being. But the book only activates when non-human entity described is [I]non[/I]-sapient? And in the rewrite, the non-human entity is the one discriminating against the human entity? But it generates sympathy for the non-human entity, despite that entity being the bad guy in this circumstance?

Relevant lines:

SCP-3726's anomalous properties manifest when any narrative with at least one anthropomorphic non-sapient being (designated SCP-3726-1) and at least one human (designated SCP-3726-2) is written on SCP-3726.

discriminatory acts by SCP-3726-1 performed on SCP-3726-2

Putting that aside, I do agree that some examples of the altered narrative would be interesting. The article needs a bit more meat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the idea interesting but as of now it feels a little bare, I feel some experiment log might add to this article.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Second scip, took three weeks to think of a viable idea, one hour to write and about two hours to review and edit.

Massive thanks to SpookyPizza, Sarangsi, Tufto, Nekromykon, MaliceAforethought, SoullessSingularity and A Vacuum for looking through this in chat!

Crosslink to SCP-3150 given permission by Tufto. Check it out :)

Image link: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Codex_-_Upper_cover_(IC1425).jpg
